case_name: Bachan Singh v. State of Punjab
facts: Constitutional validity of the death penalty under Section 302 IPC read with Section 354(3) CrPC was challenged. The question was whether the death penalty was violative of Articles 14, 19 and 21 of the Constitution.
held: The death penalty is constitutionally valid but must be imposed only in the 'rarest of rare' cases where the alternative of life imprisonment is unquestionably foreclosed. The court laid down guidelines for when death penalty may be imposed.
legal_principle: Death penalty permissible only in 'rarest of rare' cases — a case which, when observed, the collective conscience of the community is so shocked that it expects holders of judicial power to inflict death penalty.

case_name: Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India
facts: Petitioner's passport was impounded by the Government without giving her any opportunity to be heard. The question was whether this violated her fundamental rights under Articles 14, 19 and 21.
held: The right to live is not merely confined to physical existence but includes the right to live with human dignity. The procedure established by law must be fair, just and reasonable. The three Articles 14, 19 and 21 are not mutually exclusive and must be read together.
legal_principle: Right to life under Article 21 includes right to live with dignity. Any law depriving life/liberty must be fair, just, and reasonable. Articles 14, 19, and 21 form a golden triangle.

case_name: K.M. Nanavati v. State of Maharashtra
facts: Naval Commander Nanavati shot and killed Prem Ahuja, his wife's lover, after confronting him. He was tried by jury which returned a verdict of not guilty, but the Sessions Judge referred the case to the High Court. The High Court convicted him.
held: The Supreme Court upheld the conviction for murder under Section 302 IPC. The concept of 'grave and sudden provocation' was not applicable as the accused had time to think and plan. The verdict led to the abolition of the jury system in India.
legal_principle: Provocation must be grave AND sudden. If the accused had time to cool down and plan the act, the defence of provocation fails. Exception 1 to Section 300 IPC strictly interpreted.

case_name: Hussainara Khatoon v. State of Bihar
facts: A newspaper article revealed that thousands of undertrial prisoners in Bihar were languishing in jails for periods longer than the maximum sentence for the offences they were charged with. A writ petition was filed for their release.
held: Right to speedy trial is a fundamental right under Article 21. Undertrial prisoners cannot be kept in jail for periods longer than the maximum sentence for the offence. Courts must ensure speedy justice. The concept of public interest litigation was significantly developed.
legal_principle: Speedy trial is an essential component of right to life and liberty under Article 21. Indefinite detention of undertrials is unconstitutional. PIL was recognized as an important tool for constitutional remedy.

case_name: Vishaka v. State of Rajasthan
facts: Bhanwari Devi, a social worker, was gang-raped for preventing a child marriage. The case highlighted the absence of legislation protecting women from sexual harassment at the workplace.
held: In the absence of legislation, the Supreme Court issued 'Vishaka Guidelines' for protection of women against sexual harassment at the workplace. These guidelines were made legally binding until appropriate legislation was enacted. The POSH Act 2013 later codified these guidelines.
legal_principle: Employer has constitutional duty to protect female employees from sexual harassment. In absence of legislation, judicial guidelines are binding under Article 141. The Vishaka Guidelines became the foundation of POSH Act 2013.

case_name: State of Maharashtra v. Madhurkar Narayan Mardikar
facts: A police officer was acquitted of charges of rape despite victim's testimony. The sessions court and high court held that the victim was a woman of 'easy virtue' and hence the officer was acquitted.
held: Even a woman of easy virtue is entitled to privacy and no one can violate it. Past sexual conduct of a woman cannot be used to discredit her testimony in rape cases. The right to privacy and dignity is inviolable.
legal_principle: A woman's past sexual conduct is irrelevant to the question of whether rape occurred. Every woman, regardless of her character or occupation, has a right to dignity and bodily integrity.

case_name: Arnesh Kumar v. State of Bihar
facts: The petitioner was sought to be arrested by his wife under IPC Section 498A without proper investigation. The question was whether arrest could be made mechanically in cases under Section 498A.
held: Police officers cannot arrest accused automatically in cases under IPC 498A. Magistrates cannot authorize detention mechanically. A check-list of conditions must be satisfied before arrest. The judgment aimed to prevent misuse of Section 498A.
legal_principle: Arrest is not automatic; it is last resort. Police and magistrates must apply their minds. Seven categories from Section 41(1)(b) CrPC must be considered. Section 498A should not be weaponized.

case_name: Lalita Kumari v. Government of UP
facts: Constitutional validity of mandatory registration of FIR under Section 154 CrPC was questioned. Several High Courts had taken different views on whether registration of FIR was mandatory.
held: Registration of FIR is mandatory if the information discloses a cognizable offence. Police have no discretion to conduct preliminary inquiry in such cases. However, in cases where prima facie no cognizable offence is made out, preliminary inquiry may be conducted.
legal_principle: FIR registration is mandatory for cognizable offences under Section 154 CrPC. Police officer has no discretion to refuse registration. Preliminary inquiry permitted only in limited categories.

case_name: Sharad Birdhichand Sarda v. State of Maharashtra
facts: The accused was convicted for murder of his wife based purely on circumstantial evidence. The question was the standard of proof required in cases based on circumstantial evidence.
held: The court laid down five golden rules ('Panchasheel') for conviction based on circumstantial evidence: 1. The circumstances from which the conclusion of guilt is to be drawn must be fully established; 2. The facts so established must be consistent only with the hypothesis of guilt; 3. The circumstances must be of a conclusive nature; 4. They must exclude every possible hypothesis except the one to be proved; 5. There must be a chain of evidence so complete as not to leave any reasonable doubt.
legal_principle: The Panchasheel test: For conviction on circumstantial evidence, the chain of circumstances must be so complete that it points unerringly to the guilt of the accused and excludes all other reasonable hypotheses.

case_name: Mohammed Ahmed Khan v. Shah Bano Begum
facts: Shah Bano, a 73-year-old Muslim woman, was divorced by her husband and sought maintenance under Section 125 CrPC. The question was whether Section 125 CrPC applied to Muslim divorced women.
held: Section 125 CrPC is applicable to all women regardless of religion. Muslim divorced women are entitled to maintenance beyond the iddat period if they are unable to maintain themselves. This decision was subsequently overruled by the Muslim Women (Protection of Rights on Divorce) Act, 1986.
legal_principle: Section 125 CrPC is a secular provision applicable to all women. Courts have duty to apply uniform law regardless of religion. Rights of women to maintenance transcend religious personal laws.

case_name: Naz Foundation v. Government of NCT of Delhi
facts: The constitutional validity of Section 377 IPC (unnatural offences) was challenged as violating the fundamental rights of LGBT persons. The petition was filed by Naz Foundation, an NGO.
held: Section 377 IPC insofar as it criminalizes consensual sexual acts of adults in private, is violative of Articles 14, 15, 19 and 21 of the Constitution. The right to dignity, equality and privacy cannot be denied on the basis of sexual orientation.
legal_principle: Sexual orientation is an inherent and immutable part of identity. Consensual private adult acts cannot be criminalized. Constitutional morality must override social morality.

case_name: K.S. Puttaswamy v. Union of India
facts: Constitutional validity of Aadhaar was challenged. Preliminary issue was whether right to privacy is a fundamental right under the Constitution. The government argued that there is no fundamental right to privacy based on earlier judgments.
held: Right to Privacy is a fundamental right under Article 21 of the Constitution. The earlier judgments of M.P. Sharma and Kharak Singh (which held to the contrary) were overruled. Privacy includes bodily integrity, personal autonomy, informational privacy, and decisional autonomy.
legal_principle: Privacy is a fundamental right. It encompasses informational privacy, bodily integrity, and personal autonomy. State must justify any intrusion into privacy by demonstrating legality, legitimate aim, and proportionality.

case_name: Indira Sawhney v. Union of India
facts: The Mandal Commission Report recommending 27% reservation for OBCs in central government services was challenged. Various aspects of the reservation policy including the 50% cap were considered.
held: Reservations for OBCs is constitutionally valid but total reservations cannot exceed 50% except in extraordinary circumstances. 'Creamy layer' concept applied — advanced members of OBC community should be excluded from reservations. Reservations cannot be applied to promotions.
legal_principle: Total reservation cannot exceed 50% rule. Creamy layer concept: Economically advanced members of OBC should not get reservation benefits. Reservation is a tool for equalization, not a permanent feature.

case_name: D.K. Basu v. State of West Bengal
facts: A letter written by an advocate highlighting deaths in police custody was treated as a writ petition. The question was what safeguards should be provided to persons arrested by police.
held: The court laid down 11 specific requirements to be followed in all cases of arrest and detention: memo of arrest, informing relatives, medical examination, right to meet advocate, etc. Custodial torture is a violation of Article 21.
legal_principle: Custodial death and torture are grave violations of fundamental rights. Police must follow strict procedural safeguards during arrest. Violation of these safeguards would not only affect prosecution case but also render officers liable.

case_name: Pratap Dixit v. State of UP
facts: The accused was convicted for attempt to murder. The question was whether the provisions for bail under Section 439 CrPC were correctly applied by the courts below.
held: While granting bail, courts must consider: (1) the nature and gravity of accusation; (2) antecedents of the accused; (3) possibility of accused fleeing justice; (4) possibility of tampering with evidence; (5) safety of society. Bail is rule, jail is exception for bailable offences.
legal_principle: Bail jurisprudence: Courts must balance personal liberty with societal interest. Factors for bail include flight risk, tampering, gravity of offence. Personal liberty under Article 21 must be respected.

case_name: State of Rajasthan v. Balchand
facts: The accused was convicted and sentenced to imprisonment. The question was whether bail could be granted pending appeal and what factors should guide the court.
held: Bail is the rule and jail is the exception. The court should lean in favour of granting bail unless there are compelling circumstances to justify detention. The purpose of bail is to ensure appearance at trial, not to punish.
legal_principle: Bail is the rule, jail is exception — landmark statement by Justice V.R. Krishna Iyer. Courts should not deny bail mechanically. Personal liberty is precious and not to be compromised without compelling reasons.

case_name: Moti Ram v. State of Madhya Pradesh
facts: An undertrial prisoner could not furnish surety for bail due to poverty. The question was whether a poor person could be denied bail merely because he could not afford surety.
held: A poor person cannot be denied bail merely because he cannot furnish surety. The court has power to release a person on personal bond without sureties. The bail provisions must be applied in a manner consistent with Article 21.
legal_principle: Poverty cannot be a reason to deny bail. Courts have power to grant bail on personal recognizance without sureties. Access to justice cannot be denied to the poor.

case_name: R v. R (Husband's Immunity from Prosecution for Rape)
facts: A husband was charged with rape and attempted rape of his wife during the breakdown of their marriage. The husband relied on the old common law rule that a husband could not be guilty of raping his wife.
held: The old common law fiction that a wife on marriage gives irrevocable consent to sexual intercourse by her husband was abolished. A husband can be convicted of raping his wife. The marital rape exception is inconsistent with modern family law and human rights.
legal_principle: Marital rape is a crime. There is no immunity for spouses from sexual assault charges. This case influenced Indian debates on marital rape, which remains a contested issue in India.

case_name: Joginder Kumar v. State of UP
facts: An advocate was arrested and detained without reason or notice to family members. The case highlighted arbitrary police arrests.
held: No arrest can be made merely because it is lawful for the police to do so. There must be justification for making the arrest. The arrested person has the right to be informed of the grounds of arrest and to meet his lawyer. Family/friends must be informed of the arrest.
legal_principle: Arrest power must be exercised with care and circumspection. Police officer is accountable for every arrest. Rights of arrested person include: knowing reasons for arrest, right to advocate, informing relatives.

case_name: Suresh Kumar Koushal v. Naz Foundation
facts: The Supreme Court was hearing an appeal against the Delhi High Court's 2009 judgment which had decriminalized consensual same-sex acts between adults under Section 377 IPC.
held: The Supreme Court reversed the Delhi High Court judgment and held that Section 377 IPC is constitutionally valid. The Court held that a 'miniscule minority' cannot be used to strike down a law. This judgment was subsequently overruled in Navtej Singh Johar (2018).
legal_principle: Subsequently overruled — minority rights cannot be disregarded. This case demonstrates how the Supreme Court can revisit and overrule its own decisions in light of evolving constitutional values.

case_name: Navtej Singh Johar v. Union of India
facts: Section 377 IPC criminalizing consensual same-sex acts between adults was challenged. The petitioners included several prominent persons from the LGBTQ community.
held: Consensual same-sex acts between adults are decriminalized. Section 377 IPC is read down to exclude consensual acts between adults. The Suresh Kumar Koushal judgment was overruled. Sexual orientation is an inherent part of identity.
legal_principle: Constitutional morality supersedes social morality. LGBTQ persons have equal rights to dignity, privacy and equality. Consent and adult autonomy are central to constitutional freedoms.

case_name: Gian Kaur v. State of Punjab
facts: Constitutional validity of Sections 306 (abetment of suicide) and 309 (attempt to suicide) IPC was challenged on the ground that they violated Article 21.
held: Right to life under Article 21 does not include right to die. Section 309 IPC (attempt to suicide) is constitutional. Right to die with dignity is different from right to die. Euthanasia and assisted suicide remain a separate question.
legal_principle: Right to life under Article 21 does not include right to extinguish it. Suicide and its abetment are criminal acts. Right to die with dignity (natural death) was later recognized in Aruna Shanbaug case.

case_name: State of Haryana v. Bhajan Lal
facts: The question was whether the High Court can quash an FIR and when such power should be exercised under Section 482 CrPC.
held: The Supreme Court laid down seven categories of cases where FIR/criminal proceedings may be quashed: (1) Allegations do not constitute any offence; (2) Allegations are absurd; (3) Where there is a legal bar; (4) Where proceedings are mala fide; etc.
legal_principle: FIR can be quashed under Section 482 CrPC in exceptional circumstances. The power of High Court to quash FIR is wide but should be exercised sparingly. Seven categories of quashable FIRs were enumerated.

case_name: Sushil Kumar Sharma v. Union of India
facts: A petition was filed questioning the misuse of IPC Section 498A by disgruntled wives to harass husbands and their families with false cases. Statistics showed high rates of acquittal and filing of false cases.
held: The existence of Section 498A and its misuse cannot be a ground to strike it down. However, the court acknowledged the misuse and directed that police should be careful in making arrests. The law is for protection of women and cannot be used as a tool for vengeance.
legal_principle: A law that is valid cannot be struck down merely because it is misused. However, misuse of Section 498A to harass innocent family members must be checked. Courts must guard against false cases.

case_name: P. Ramachandra Rao v. State of Karnataka
facts: The question was whether courts can fix time limits for completion of criminal trials as a remedy for violation of the right to speedy trial under Article 21.
held: Courts cannot fix a blanket time limit for completion of criminal trials. However, in individual cases, courts can take steps to expedite trial. The right to speedy trial is a fundamental right but it must be balanced with the needs of justice.
legal_principle: No blanket time limit for criminal trials but right to speedy trial must be ensured case by case. Delayed trial is a violation of Article 21. Remedy is within the legal framework, not by fixing arbitrary time limits.
